topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a familiar chime from 'Starlight_Voyager.' I clicked it, expecting a witty comment about the latest sci-fi movie. Instead, the profile picture resolved into a grainy, slightly out-of-focus photo of a brick wall I recognized instantly—the one beside the old public library downtown. My breath hitched. I reread the caption, a slow, cold dread creeping up my spine. "Guess who lives in the same zip code?" My fingers froze above the keyboard. I glanced around my cramped apartment, the afternoon sun slanting through the window, suddenly feeling too bright, too exposed. I stood up, pacing the small living room, the floorboards protesting under my weight. Every sound seemed amplified—the distant siren, the hum of the refrigerator—and my heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, trapped bird.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed, trying to distract myself from the dull ache of loneliness, when I saw it. A profile I’d only interacted with in the comments section suddenly featured a geolocation tag that matched my current location. My hand trembled as I zoomed in. It was a photo of a coffee shop across the street from my building. A sudden, sharp intake of air escaped my lungs. I dropped my phone onto the worn sofa. The silence of my room became heavy, pressing in on me. I started checking the windows, peering through the blinds, half-expecting to see someone lurking in the alley below. My palms grew slick against the cool glass of the windowpane. I stood rigidly by the door, listening intently for any sound that wasn't my own ragged breathing, a knot tightening painfully in my stomach.

[story 3]

It started innocently enough—a shared inside joke about a local landmark. Then came the subtle shifts. A mention of a specific bus route, a reference to a neighborhood park I frequented. Now, the reality of it hit me like a physical blow. I was looking at a profile picture, and the backdrop was undeniably my own street. My gaze darted from the screen to the window, a frantic sweep of my eyes searching for an anomaly, a sign that this connection was misplaced. A sudden wave of nausea washed over me. I gripped the edge of the desk so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every casual interaction with my phone now felt loaded, weighted with an unspoken proximity that made the air feel thin and difficult to draw into my chest.
